sentences.text: My name is Michael Okunieff.
And the spelling is Michael, M-I-C-H-A-E-L, last name Okunieff, O-K-U-N-I-E-F-F.
Moshe Okunw.
Moshe, M-O-S-H-E, Okunw, O-K-U-N-W.
No.
Mike.
I was born in Vilna.
V-I-L-N-A, Poland.
When I start to remind myself about the past, I feel like a chill goes through my body.
I see before myself the town where I grew up and the beauty, and the enthusiasm.
We grew up, and this suddenly disappeared from the face of Earth.
The whole community disappear.
And there remains only a echo and a reminder.
This was a beautiful youth what I grew up.
I grew up.
My family consisted of three brothers and parents.
My father's name was Pinchas.
He was born-- he came in from Moscow during the revolution in 1920 and was born on the turn of the century, in 1900.
And he run away from the Bolsheviks.
And he came in to Vilna-- Vilna, where-- where he married my mother, [NON-ENGLISH], in 1901.
They had three children.
One, Beryl, was the oldest one.
I was the middle one.
And the youngest one was Gershon.
My older brother was born in 1920.
I was born in 1923.
And my younger brother was born in 1928.
The youth was pleasant.
We had-- what I still remember about my town is I can still hear when there was a Sunday.
You could hear the high-- the bells were ringing.
And this was like a holiday.
The town Vilna was a cross-section of cultures.
It was on the crossroad of-- of-- of states.
Napoleon in 1912 came through.

In addition, as was a cross-current of cultures, as I mentioned before, from one side, if you go out in Vilna, you could see a beautiful cathedral with-- in the center of the city, built on the-- on the-- due to the influence of the Italian Renaissance and built by the Polish szlachta.
I will come to it.
The second one [? we ?] [? saw ?] the other cultures.
And normally, we see the Gothic and the big-- the big cross-section and the-- and the [? church is ?] [?
Anne. ?] In addition, we saw the influence of the Russian-- Russian culture, the Byzantine.
And we see over there the-- the St.
Peters church.
The Jewish-- the Jewish shuls were not-- you couldn't see them.
Why?
Because most of them were underground.
And according to the law of the land, they had to be built lower than the churches and not to have-- not to expose themself.
They were built mostly as-- as over houses or were located over houses or underground.
The most famous of them was the-- the Great Shul.
The Great Shul was where it was located is second ghetto.
This was a shul of the Gaon.
The Gaon of Vilna.
Talmud. ?] And the thing is it was about five flights underneath.
The center, it was built still was a replica of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the temple.
And it's considered one of the most beautiful structures in Europe.
And in the middle, it was a bema.
And it's had the same-- it's has same pedestals.
And it had the same-- the-- it's had the different-- different architecture-- the Corinthian-- a mixture of Corinthian and architecture imported from the Italian-- Italy.
But in addition, it's had some facades all around.
I was only about two or three times.
The thing is most of the time, I was-- that I'm young.
And the-- the-- it was very-- it's very hard to go in over there.
First of all, it's was-- it's was only the-- the synagogues.
The house of worship were mostly attended by the same people.
We were live-- we were living quite a-- quite a way from over there.
And the-- the services-- usually, you used to go down near-- near the house.
Yeah.
Right.
Right.
OK.
But the house-- Vilna was a combination.
And normally, before I'm going to talk about this one, I would like to talk-- the only structure what in Vilna what was above the ground was the synagogue.
It was the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], this was already built later.
This was a round-- the-- the-- the round windows.
And under the influence of the-- in the 19, almost 20th century, the-- this was-- the pressure on the Jews was diminished.
And they were allowed already to build some structure over the ground.
And probably they got it-- they got it, probably, permission from the authorities what I was talking about, later.
But if you see in Vilna, you have-- I mention in here, Vilna was the center, the culture of the Jewry.
You have the 18th century.
You have on one side, you have Zalman, the Great Gaon, with the introduction of the Mitnagdim, the introduction of the [? scholar, ?] with-- with the introduction of Zohar.
From the other side, you have Shneur Zalman-- Shneur Zalman of Liady, with the Hasidim.
And it was a continuous fraction between them.
And it was not-- it was to-- to a point almost that this was-- this was almost to a war.
But in-- in-- in the-- in the conflict, in the dialect, you have-- you developed the survival of the Jewry because from this moment, we have [NON-ENGLISH].
But haved later.
This is the 18th century. 19th century, we have the [? scholar. ?] We have-- we have [? medicine. ?] We have what-- what probably gives the Jews the Jewish Yiddish language.
And by the 20th century, but Vilna, we have-- we have usually the labor-- development of labor, developed the Hebrew-- Hebrew language, and the Zionism.
Vilna was at center.
Vilna had the seminars.
Vilna had-- Vilna had the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
Vilna collected-- collected practically the all was the Yerushalayim, the leader.
Yeah.
The Jew-- the-- its youth was politically oriented.
The Jews were continuously almost-- almost an inspiration.
We didn't have drugs.
We have the-- the-- the-- the idea of us was-- was continuously discussions of the-- about the life.
Imagine, this little Vilna had four-- four Hebrew gymnasium-- Gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH], Tarbut, and one Yiddish gymnasium, Real Gymnasium.
In addition, it's had 12 gymnasiums with the high schools.
In addition, a seminar what was rabbinical seminar, what was send out the rabbis all over the-- all over Poland.
In addition, they had technical schools.
They had-- they had-- a cultural life what-- what practically the [?
Jewry ?] would serve as inspiration for-- for-- for-- for the entire Jewish community.
I-- in the beginning, in the primary school, I went to the Hebrew school and was [?
Chojnow. ?] After that, in 1935, I was transferred to a public school.
After I graduated from-- from public school, I went to Gymnasium Epstein.
Now, I want to give you a few stories, a few memories from my-- from my schooling.
One of them is in 1935.
Was probably when I was 12 years old.
Was remained in my memory-- ingrained in my memory.
In this year, I transferred from Chojnow to a public school.
The [?
Chojnow ?] was the-- was the elementary--
The Hebrew.
Hebrew.
Hebrew school.
I remember all the schools-- Hebrew schools-- all the schools were supported.
The Jewish schools were supported by Vilna.
The government-- we did not have any support from the government.
Everything was all continuously, they were collecting money.
Now, in 1935, just before I transferred to the other-- to the private school, Haim Bialik came into-- to Vilna.
And he was in our school.
And I still remember this man-- the magnificent, still after the many years.
He came in.
And he was talking about Shabbos.
And he said-- and he said, we have the fire.
The Jews are burning.
[NON-ENGLISH] Just was like Jabotinsky.
We have another Jabotinsky on the horizon.
The-- the 'City of Slaughter' is coming again.
Yeah.
And at that time, just Shabbos.
But we cannot live with the reality.
We's have to lift ourself from reality to the dream, just like Shabbos.
You work just like [NON-ENGLISH] like it's a-- like it's a-- like it's a Jewish laborer, what works a whole week and sweat.
It-- the Shabbos was something where he release all his-- his-- his sorrows, his-- his-- his hardship.
And elevate yourself.
And he almost-- he lifts-- he elevated to a different stage.
He mention about Aliyah.
I don't remember everything.
I was too young at that time.
I was only 12 years old.
I would like to talk about this a little later.
But they was thinking about it.
But as was only a dream.
It's hard to leave a place where you spend all your life.
It's was hard to go to a place-- to a new place without the language.
It's was hard to go to a place where the-- where all the gates were closed.
It's-- it was impossible.
They did not want him.
They did not want us in here.
The-- the all-- the all-- the-- the all gates to the world was closed.
Definitely, they would go, especially the younger-- the younger generation.
My brother was in-- in technical-- technical school.
I was in the gymnasium.
But I was in the middle.
But the thing is, all-- all bridges were still burned in order to go.
Yiddish.
Well, it was-- it was a traditional house.
Shabbos, you had to be a-- a Shabbos was a celebration.
Shabbos was something in here, what-- what you released yourself from the-- from the pressure of the days, of-- of the week.
As Bialik said.
Oh, sure, it was something in here.
You had to find a release, like-- like the Kabbalah says.
You have to-- you-- in order to-- to get to release yourself from the pressure, you had to-- you had to celebrate.
You have to go away and to learn-- and-- and to forget.
Let me now bring a little bit the story why.
Poland was-- under Pilsudski, what was the president, was kind of liberal.
Liberal in this respect, that they did not-- they try-- Pilsudski did not try to persecute the Jews.
Starting with his death--
'36, we have-- [LAUGHS] we have Rydz-Smigly.
Rydz-Smigly was a anti-Semite.
He was under the influence of the-- of the German.
The German took over with the propaganda.
This what you call cruel propaganda-- Heilsgeschichte.
Cruel propaganda simple was in here that we are dispensable.
We are, because of us, the world cannot develop.
And we are almost like the cancer of the-- of the-- of the society.
And they had to cut it out.
And being in here, you have the-- the-- the-- the-- it's a radical element in Poland take over.
And at  that time, you have [POLISH].
You have the laws-- numerous clauses.
What is 10% only of Jews what can attend a university.
You have don't buy in Jewish stores the-- the-- and the different-- but it's on-- on the surface, the different so-called unwritten laws.
But now I want to bring two stories what happened to me.
One story is what happened at school.
I'm transferring to-- to-- to the school, to the public school.
And there was one day, it was almost late after-- after school.
We are going-- I am going out from-- from the building.
And suddenly, a group of Polish student was-- was ambushed-- ambushed me.
I was the first one to come out.
They were hiding under-- under the-- one of the stairs.
And he hit me with a metal--
Bar.
And he-- and he cut my-- it cut me over my eye.
And it gushed.
And blood started to gush in here.
And I was covered all with blood.
And they ran away.
Yeah.
And the-- the guys-- and my-- my school friends, in the meantime, came down from the stairs, saw me, and they were asking me.
And I told them the story.
And before you know it, this-- the Polish hoodlum or-- or-- there was not even hoodlums.
It was the student, they disappeared.
And they brought me to the ambulatory.
And they stitch me.
And I still have the scar.
And following the incident, we decided that-- that I'm to get even.
And one-- one day, while we were-- while we were returned from music lessons with our instruments, we met the same guys.
We put up a fight, and we-- we hit them quite-- quite bad because at that time, we were in majority.
And this was close to Passover.
And we were using so-called the Christian blood-- the blood from the Christian-- Christian children to make our matzahs.
And this was so ingrained that every thing-- that this was even propagated in the-- in-- in the church.
And at that time, we were probably a little-- we were some scared to go out from the house.
Yeah, but Christian friends [? on ?] the name.
There was no Christian friends-- really Christian friends.
In name only.
And the thing is, so to-- you have-- and I would like to describe one incident what happened to my father.
Yeah.
OK.
This-- my father-- the-- the profession, the work, the adjustment to life was dependent-- depending from the wind of-- and [LAUGHS] the wind-- what I mean from the-- from special situation.
And normally, my father had different jobs and work.
One of the-- in the beginning, he was-- he was, in 1929, I remember, he was running a mill.
It was [POLISH].
And because in-- because of the Depression, he lost it.
In 1935, or 1934, he changed his profession.
And he was administrator of the-- of the building.
My grandmother and my-- my grandfather-- and my grandfamily--
From my mother's side.
Yeah.
Had 12 children.
And we had a big, big building.
And this building-- she had 12 children.
And she divided the-- the building to-- between the children.
My grandfather build a lot of buildings in Vilna.
And this is what he left for the children.
And this-- this house was a long house.
It's extended almost close to a mile.
Extended--
Yeah.
His name-- it was Deweltow?
Deweltow.
Yeah.
Right, Deweltow.
And it's extended from [POLISH] 21 up to [POLISH] 5.
It's was 500 families over there.
And it was close to-- I'm sorry, it was 100 families over there.
It was close to 500 people.
And the-- and over there, so the thing is, he was-- he had a very nice reputation in-- in Vilna.
And Deweltow-- the family of Deweltow was very familiar.
And my father, in 1936, went into business of producing gloves and jacket-- leather jacket.
And this type of work took him all over Poland.
And one time, once upon a time, he came in back home with bandages all over his body.
And he was cut up.
He did not wanted to tell us at all, even Mother.
But after-- OK, we're-- we're insisting in here, he told us the story.
He was-- he went to Poznan-- Poznan, what is northern part of Poland.
And over there, there on the train was a group of Hasidim in the all garb-- you know, with the payots, with the-- with the-- with-- with the dress in here.
And suddenly-- suddenly, the-- a [POLISH] band-- a band-- a band of-- of [POLISH], what is the Polish hoodlum, or whatever you want to call them, came in with scissors.
